name: Anshar
description: Anshar, meaning "Whole Heaven," is a primordial deity in Mesopotamian mythology, specifically within the Babylonian creation epic, the *Enūma Eliš*. He represents the celestial firmament, the expanse of the sky itself, and is a fundamental element in the cosmogony. He is the son of Lahamu and Lahmu, the first generation of deities born from Apsu (fresh water) and Tiamat (salt water), representing silt. Anshar is considered the father of Anu, the god of the sky, making him a significant figure in the divine lineage. His consort is Kishar, representing the earth. Together, Anshar and Kishar embody the totality of the cosmos, the unified heaven and earth. He plays a crucial role in the events leading to the conflict between the younger gods and Tiamat. While not directly involved in the battle, his existence as a primordial being is essential to the narrative and the unfolding of the cosmos. His presence highlights the Mesopotamian understanding of the universe as a structured and ordered entity arising from primordial chaos.
subtitle: Whole Heaven

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Anshar and Kishar
keyMyths.description: Anshar and Kishar emerged from Lahmu and Lahamu, who themselves were born from Apsu and Tiamat. This lineage establishes Anshar as a key figure in the primordial generation of deities. His birth represents the differentiation of the cosmos from the initial chaotic waters, highlighting the beginning of order and structure. His existence is a crucial step in the creation of the world as the Mesopotamians understood it, representing the literal division of the waters above from the waters below.

keyMyths.title: Anshar's Counsel
keyMyths.description: In the face of Tiamat's rage and the threat she posed to the younger gods, Anshar plays a role as a counselor. He is approached for advice and guidance on how to confront the primordial goddess. His wisdom, derived from his position as a primordial being, is sought after. This demonstrates his importance in the divine hierarchy and his perceived knowledge of the cosmos and its origins. He helps to set the stage for Marduk's eventual victory.

keyMyths.title: The Lineage to Anu
keyMyths.description: Anshar's role as the father of Anu is significant. Anu, the sky god, represents the established order and authority of the heavens. Through Anu, Anshar's influence extends throughout the Mesopotamian pantheon. This familial connection reinforces Anshar's importance as a foundational deity whose existence is essential for the subsequent generations of gods and the establishment of cosmic order. He is a vital link in the chain of divine succession.

sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Mesopotamian myths, including the Enūma Eliš, providing valuable context and translations.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Mesopotamian myths, including the Enūma Eliš, providing valuable context and translations.

sources.author: Thorkild Jacobsen
sources.work: The Treasures of Darkness: A History of Mesopotamian Religion
sources.description: An in-depth exploration of Mesopotamian religious beliefs and practices, offering insights into the roles of deities like Anshar.
sources.text: An in-depth exploration of Mesopotamian religious beliefs and practices, offering insights into the roles of deities like Anshar.

sources.author: Benjamin R. Foster
sources.work: Before the Muses: An Anthology of Akkadian Literature
sources.description: A collection of Akkadian texts, including excerpts from the Enūma Eliš, providing access to primary source material.
sources.text: A collection of Akkadian texts, including excerpts from the Enūma Eliš, providing access to primary source material.

worshipPractices.name: Reverence through Cosmic Order
worshipPractices.description: Anshar's worship, though not explicitly detailed, was likely interwoven with maintaining the cosmic order. People revered the sky and its stability as a source of security and predictability, therefore honoring Anshar through rituals and practices that reinforced this belief.

worshipPractices.name: Astronomical Observations
worshipPractices.description: Given Anshar's association with the heavens, astronomical observations and the interpretation of celestial events may have been a form of indirect worship. Tracking the movements of stars and planets could have been seen as understanding and honoring Anshar's domain.

festivals.name: Akitu Festival (New Year Festival)
festivals.description: While not directly dedicated to Anshar, the Akitu festival celebrated the renewal of the cosmos and the triumph of order over chaos, themes closely aligned with Anshar's essence as the 'Whole Heaven'. The festival reaffirmed the stability of the heavens after the perceived threats of the winter months.

cultural.modernLegacy: Anshar's primary influence remains within academic studies of Mesopotamian mythology and comparative religion. His role as a primordial deity contributes to understanding the cosmological beliefs of ancient Babylon and informs the development of religious thought. He serves as a historical example of personifying the sky and celestial sphere within ancient cultures.

associations.name: Horizon
associations.significance: Anshar represents the entirety of the heavens, embodying the very edge and boundary of the visible sky.

associations.name: Totality
associations.significance: His name, 'Whole Heaven', directly connects him to the concept of completeness and encompassing all that is celestial.

associations.name: Air
associations.significance: As the personification of the sky, Anshar is inherently linked to the element of air and its vastness.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: The blue of the sky is often associated with Anshar, representing the visible expanse of the heavens he embodies.

associations.name: Order
associations.significance: As a primordial deity, Anshar's emergence contributes to the ordering of the cosmos from primordial chaos.

associations.name: Firmament
associations.significance: Anshar is the embodiment of the firmament, the solid vault of the sky that separates the waters above from the waters below.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: As a son of Lahmu and Lahamu, he represents a duality and the beginning of familial lineage in the cosmos.

companions.name: Kishar
companions.description: Kishar, meaning "Whole Earth," is Anshar's female counterpart and wife. Together, they represent the complete cosmic duality of Heaven and Earth.

companions.name: Anu
companions.description: Anu is the son of Anshar and Kishar, and represents the sky god. He is a key figure in the Mesopotamian pantheon, inheriting his father's dominion over the heavens.

companions.name: Ea
companions.description: Ea (also known as Enki) is the son of Anu and a major deity of wisdom, magic, and water. As Anshar's grandson, he continues the lineage of powerful celestial beings.

companions.name: Lahamu
companions.description: Lahamu is the mother of Anshar, representing the muddy waters. She helped birth the celestial heavens.

companions.name: Lahmu
companions.description: Lahmu is the father of Anshar, representing the muddy waters. He helped birth the celestial heavens.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Primordial Role
extendedContent.content: Anshar emerges in the *Enūma Eliš* as a crucial figure in the early genealogy of the gods. He is the offspring of Lahmu and Lahamu, themselves children of Apsu and Tiamat. This lineage places Anshar within the first generation of deities, marking him as a primordial being intimately connected to the very fabric of the cosmos. His name, meaning "Whole Heaven" or "Entire Sky," clearly defines his domain: the celestial sphere, the visible expanse above. In the chaotic primordial state described in the epic, Anshar represents the initial ordering of the universe, a separation and definition of space within the watery abyss of Apsu and Tiamat. This act of definition is essential for the subsequent development of the cosmos and the emergence of further deities and eventually, humanity. Without the separation represented by Anshar (and his sister/consort Kishar, representing the whole earth), the creation process could not proceed. The very concept of a structured, ordered universe hinges on Anshar's presence and role in establishing the initial boundaries.

extendedContent.title: Anshar's Significance in the Divine Hierarchy
extendedContent.content: While not as actively involved in the narrative as some of the later generations of gods, Anshar holds a position of immense importance within the Mesopotamian pantheon due to his primordial status. He is a great-grandfather of Ea (also known as Enki), the god of wisdom and magic, and thus an ancestor of Marduk, the eventual champion who defeats Tiamat and establishes the current cosmic order. This lineage underscores Anshar's indirect but crucial role in Marduk's triumph. He represents the ancient, foundational principles upon which the later, more active deities build their power and authority. Furthermore, his association with the celestial realm makes him a symbol of cosmic order and stability. Although he doesn't directly intervene in the conflict between the younger gods and Tiamat, his existence provides a backdrop of established cosmic structure against which the conflict plays out. He is a passive but essential element, a reminder of the ancient forces that shaped the universe.

extendedContent.title: Anshar's Role in the *Enūma Eliš* Narrative
extendedContent.content: Anshar, despite his significance, plays a relatively passive role in the active events of the *Enūma Eliš*. He is primarily present as a figure of authority and ancestry, consulted by the younger gods during the crisis precipitated by Tiamat's rebellion. When the gods are distraught by Tiamat's preparations for war, they turn to Anshar for guidance and support. He, in turn, sends his messenger, Gaga, to consult with other deities and rally them to the cause. This act, while not directly combative, is crucial in mobilizing the divine forces against Tiamat. Anshar essentially acts as a figurehead, a symbol of legitimacy and ancient power, lending his weight to the effort to find a champion capable of defeating Tiamat. His involvement highlights the respect and deference owed to the primordial gods, even by the newer generations. This respect underlines the Mesopotamian belief in the importance of ancestry and tradition, even as they embraced innovation and change.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Interpretations
extendedContent.content: Anshar's legacy extends beyond his specific role in the *Enūma Eliš*. As the embodiment of the "Whole Heaven," he represents the Mesopotamian understanding of the cosmos as an ordered and structured entity. His name and concept reflect a worldview that sought to understand and explain the origins of the universe and the place of humanity within it. In art and iconography, Anshar is not typically depicted directly. Instead, his presence is implied through representations of the celestial sphere and the ordering of the cosmos. Scholars interpret Anshar as a personification of the primordial separation between heaven and earth, a critical step in the creation process. He embodies the concept of defined space and the establishment of boundaries within the chaos of the primordial waters. His existence highlights the Mesopotamian emphasis on order and structure as fundamental principles of the universe and society. The continued presence of Anshar in later Mesopotamian thought, even if only as a distant ancestor, demonstrates the enduring power of the creation myths and their influence on Mesopotamian culture and religion.
